reaction

reaction reaction-tag : n1 r1 + n2 r2 + ... + nk rk  ->  m1 p1 + m2 p2 + ... + mj rj @ rate

This is easier to understand by example:

small-molecule Na 22.99
small-molecule H2O 18.0
small-molecule NaOH 39.98
small-molecule H2 2.01
reaction make-lye : 2 Na + 2 H20 -> 2 NaOH + 1 H2 @ 100.0

Note that 1 is NOT assumed as a coeffiecient if none is given; for simplicity in parsing, the coefficient must always be given. Note also that coefficients are separated from their molecules by whitespace, as usual for the primitive command parser. The rate given after the @-sign is the usual molar reaction rate.

An arrow reaction need not have any substrates (left-hand members) in which case the molar reaction rate tells how fast to produce the products out of nothing. (To create molecules out of nothing instantly, use the create command at simulation run time.) Likewise, an arrow reaction need not have any products, in which case the molar reaction rate tells how fast to destroy the substrates. This makes arrow reactions a useful way to introduce or remove substances gradually.

Note that, as implied by the above discussion, the equation is NOT checked for mass-balance. In fact, the molecular weights of molecules are not used at all in this kind of reaction: the reaction formula generally defines just one reaction, and the molecular weights are already at work in its specified reaction rate. (By contrast, the dimer command defines two families of reactions, a family of dimerization reactions and a family of decomposition reactions. The dimerizations are between the pairs of species of complexes that display the sites as free binding sites. The molecular weight given in the mol command serves as the basis for extrapolation of the given dimerization rate to all these different dimerization reactions.)

Note that complexes can participate in reactions defined in this way. Each complex has a default state; when a complex must be produced as the product of an "arrow" reaction, it is produced in this default state. Complexes made with the create command are also produced in this default state. When a complex must be destroyed as the result of an "arrow" reaction, a random "victim" is chosen from the population of all complexes with the given structure. In the following example, the complex is introduced at a constant rate:

The reaction-tag can be used in a trace command to trace the reaction.

mol foo 100.0 to-bar to-fiz
mol bar 100.0 to-foo

plex foobar
mols foo1:foo bar1:bar
bind foo1:to-bar bar1:to-foo

reaction make-foobar :  -> 1 foobar @ 1000.0
